California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 21,
1992
Amy Greyson
BRUNICK,
ALVAREZ
&
BATTERSBY
1839
Commercenter West
Post Office
Box
6425
San Bernardino,
CA
92412
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File
No.
1—92—449
Dear Ms.
Greyson:
This letter is
in response to your request for advice
regarding the responsibilities of Mojave Water Agency
Board of
Directors members John Russell
and George Parker under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”).
1
Your request is general in nature and does not refer to
a pending decision before the members of the board.
Accordingly,
we treat your letter as
a request for informal assistance pursuant
to the provisions of Regulation
18329.2
QUESTION
Under the provisions of the Act,
may Board Members Russell
and Parker participate
in governmental decisions regarding pending
litigation?
CONCLUSION
Under the provisions of the Act,
Board Members Russell and
Parker may not participate in governmental decisions regarding
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided
by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114;
Regulation 18329(c).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-92-449
Page
2
pending litigatjo if the decisions will have a material financial
effect on any of their economic interests,
as discussed below.
John Russell and George Parker are members of the Board of
Directors of the Mojave Water Agency
(HMWAII),
a public agency.
1.
Litigatj
In Nay,
1990,
a complaint for declaratory and Ifljunctjv
relief,
and
a petition for writ of mandamus
were filed in the San
Bernardino County Superior Court entitled City of arstow and the
one of
150 defendansjnis
action.
By their action,
the plaintiffs
who use water from the
Nojave River System,
sought to have the court determine the
amounts of water from the Mojave River System that the City of
Barstow and Southern California Water Company are entitled to have
flowing to them through the Barstow Gauging Statjo.
In June,
1991,
MWA filed
a cross_complaint entitled ve
v.
Anderson
et a)..,
in the above action.
A first
amended cross_complaint was then filed in July,
1991.
The named
cross_defendants in the cross_complaint include Board Member
Parker.
The purpose of MWA’s cross_complaint is to obtain an
adjudicaj
0 of water rights Within
MWA and the Mojave River area
of influence.
This adjudjcatio will affect the rights of all
persons who
own Property in the area affected and who will extract
water for their personal
or business use.
Each extractor of water
will have to pay for replacement water to recharge what they
produce in excess of safe yield.
The water rights of a cross-
defendant may be diminished to a Percentage of what he or she
claims at the present time.
The financial effect of the
adjudjcaj
0 will be to provide for payment on
a net basis for any
water extracted beyond the individual user’s base Percentage right
of the total safe yield.
2.
oard Member Parker
Board Member Parker
Owns and lives on
a iSO-acre farm in the
MWA service area.
He farms between 20-25 acres at any one time.
As
a result of the adjudication
he may have to cut back his water
usage and thus the number of acres he farms.
3.
Board Member Russell
Board Member Russell is not
a defendant or cross_defendant in
the pending action.
Mr.
Russell is
a member of the Board of
Directors of Center Water Company,
a nonprofit water company


Our File No.
1-92-449
Page
3
located in Lucerne Valley.
Center Water Company is
a small water
company that pumps approximately lO—acre feet of water per year.
Board Member Russell
owns five shares in Center Water Company.
His real Property interests within the company’s service area
consist of one duplex and one vacant lot each worth in excess of
$1,000.
He is also part owner of the land
and bildigs where the
Lucerne Valley Shopping Center is located.
The land is owned by
a
Partnership in which he is
a general partner.
His Ownership
interest in this Partnership amounts to approximately one acre.
A family trust owns the remaining portion of the shopping
center land.
Mr.
Russell has
a one—third interest in this trust.
Mr. Russell’s investment in thjs Partnership exceeds $1,000.
The
shopping center leases the buildings from this Partnership.
Mr.
Russell is also
a member of the Board of Directors of
Jubilee Mutual Water Company,
a nonprofit water company located
about four miles east of Lucerne Valley.
He owns
a
1—1/4 acre
parcel Within the service area of this company and
Owns one share
in the company which is worth approximately $100.
This share is
appurtenant to the Property and Board Member Russell believes that
the share has no negotia value.
Mr.
Russell
and his wife also own one share in the Bar-H
Mutual Water Company.
This share is based on their ownership of
one residential lot within the water company’s service area.
There are
a total of
48 parcels Within the company’s service area.
Each parcel has one vote.
Two of these water companies in Which
Mr.
Russell has an interest have been named as Roes Defendants to
the
MWA cross—complaint
Mr.
Russell
owns and resides in
a 4
0—acre parcel in Lucerne
Valley.
This Property uses under 10-acre feet of water each year.
You have Stated in your request for advice that it is
anticipated that the adjudicatjo of water rights in the cross-
complaint may increase residential water rates,
although the
amount of the increase
if any,
cannot be determined at this time.
However,
the adjudjcatj
0 of water rights will have little effect
on the water rates paid
by the shopping center because the Use is
commercial
Section 87100 prohibits Public Officials from making,
Participating in making,
or Otherwise using their official
Position to influence
a government decisjo in Which they have a
financial interest.
As members of the Board of Directors of the
Mojave Water Agency,
a public entity,
Mr.
Russell and Mr.
Parker
are public officials
(Section 82048.)


Our File No.
1-92-449
Page
4
Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that an official has
a financial
interest in a governmental decision,
within the meaning of Section
87100,
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally,
on the official,
or
on
a member of the
official’s immediate family
3 or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has
a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has
a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of
income,
other than gifts
and other than loans
by
a commercial
lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is
a director, officer,
partner,
trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for
a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public
official within
12
months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section,
indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned
by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalfof
a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official,
the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially
a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103.
An official’s
“immediate family” are his or her spouse and
dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


Our File No.
1—92-449
Page
5
1.
Board Member Russell
Mr. Russell’s
economic interests are as follows:
(1)
Mr.
Russell
owns shares in Center Water Company,
Jubilee Mutual Water Company,
and Bar-H Mutual Water Company.
You
have advised us that the value of Mr. Russell’s share in the
Jubilee Mutual Water Company is worth approximately $100.
While
these shares in nonprofit water agencies are not an investment,
they are assets with
a distinctive value.
Section 87103 requires
an official’s disqualification from decisions which would
materially affect him.
(Einboden Advice Letter,
No.
A-87-193.)
Thus,
these shares constitute potentially disqualifying economic
interests.
(2)
Mr.
Russell
owns several parcels of land,
each
presumably worth in excess of $1,000,
in areas served by these
three water companies.
(3)
Mr.
Russell has an interest
in
a partnership which
exceeds $1,000.
(4)
Mr.
Russell has an interest in a family trust which
an interest in the above partnership.
Consequently,
Mr. Russell may not participate in any
governmental decision regarding the pending litigation if it is
reasonably foreseeable that any of his economic interests will be
affected materially by the decisions.
4
2.
Board Member Parker
Board Member Parker’s economic interests are as follows:
(1)
He has an interest in real property in
a
180—acre
farm located within the
MWA service area.
(2)
He grows and sells horse hay in his farm.
For
purposes of our analysis,
we presume that he has an interest in
this business which is worth $1,000 or more.
Therefore,
he is
operating a business
in the farm.
Mr.
Russell is also a member of the board of directors of
Center Water Company and Jubilee Mutual Water Company,
which are
nonprofit water companies.
For purposes of our discussion,
we
assume that Mr.
Russell does not receive any income from his
services and thus,
because these water companies are not “business
entities” for purposes of the Act,
they do not constitute
potentially disqualifying econouiic interests.


Our File No.
I—92-449
Page
6
(3)
The hay is sold to one customer
This customer is
a Source of Iflcome to Mr.
Parker in excess of
$250
in
a calendar
year.
litigation
(4)
Mr.
Parker is
a cross_defendant in the Pending
Accordingly
he may not participate in any government
decjsjo Which will have
a material financial effect on any of his
economic interests
Makjn
Partjci
atm
in Makjn
or Attern tin
to Influence a
Pyernment Decision
A Public Officiai makes
a governrnet decision or
participates in the making of a governmen decision Whenever the
Public official Votes
on a matter
commits the agency to a course
of action,
or enters into any contractual agreeme
Ofl behalf of
the agency.
(Regulatj
0 lS?oo(b))
Additionally
a Public
official participates in
a governmen decision when,
acting
Within the authority of his or her Position,
the public Official:
(1)
Negotia
5
Without signifjca
Substantive review,
with a governmen entity or
private person regarding the decision;
or
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision_maker
either directly or without
Signifjc intervening substantive review,
by:
(A)
Conducting research or making any
investigatj
0 Which reguir
5 the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or designated
employee and the purpose of which is to influence
the decision;
or
(B)
Preparing or Presenting any report,
analysis or Opinion,
orally or in Writing,
which
requires the exercise of judgment
on the part of
the Official or designat employee and the purpose
of Which is to influence the decision.
Regulatj
0 l8700(c).
With regard to a governmefl decision Which is Within or
before an offjcja1s agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency,
an official is
attempting to use his or her official Position to influence the
decjsjo if,
for the purpose of influencing the decision,
the
5
For purposes of our analysis,
we assume that this customer is
an individual and not
a business entity.


Our File No.
1-92-449
Page
7
official contacts,
or appears before,
or otherwise attempts to
influence any member,
officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include,
but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts
by the official
on behalf of
a business
entity, client,
or customer.
(Regulation 18700.1.)
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the
board members’
economic interests will be materially affected by
governmental decisions regarding the pending litigation,
the board
members must not only disqualify themselves from participating in
formal decisions of the
MWA board which may affect their economic
interests,
but they must also abstain from attempting to influence
the decisions
by communicating with other members of the board or
with staff regarding the decisions.
Foreseeability
The effect of
a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
To be
foreseeable,
the effect of
a decision must be more than a mere
possibility;
however,
certainty is not required.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development
Comm.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989—
991; Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.App.3d 817;
In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest.
(Witt v.
Morrow,
suora at 823.)
The pending litigation will affect the water rights and the
cost of water for all property owners in the area affected by the
litigation.
Both board members
own land which will be affected by
the outcome of the pending litigation.
Mr.
Russell also has
investments in water companies which will be affected by the
outcome of the litigation.
In addition,
Mr.
Parker is
a cross—
defendant in the litigation and may have to incur costs in
defending the lawsuit.
Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable
that decisions regarding the pending litigation will have an
effect on the board members’
economic interests.
If the effect is
material, disqualification will be required unless the “public
generally” exception applies.
Materiality
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official’s economic interest in
a decision is
materially affected by a decision.
If the official’s economic
interest is directly involved in the decision,
Regulation 18702.1
provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality.
Regulation 18702.1(b)
states that a person or business entity is
directly involved in a decision before an official’s agency when
that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will
be made by filing an application,
claim,
appeal,
or similar request or;


Our File No.
1-92-449
Page
8
(2)
Is
a
named party in,
or is the subject
of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official’s agency.
(3)
A person or business entity is the
subject of
a proceeding if
a decision involves the
issuance,
renewal,
approval, denial or revocation
of any license,
permit,
or other entitlement to,
or
contract with,
the subject person or business
entity.
Emphasis added.
Board Member Parker
1.
Real Property
The pending litigation will affect Mr.
Parker’s interests in
real property indirectly.
When an official’s interest in real
property will be affected indirectly by pending governmental
decisions,
and where there is no subject property from which
distances can be measured,
the effect of the decision is material
if the decision will have
a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more
on
the fair market value of the real property
in which
the official has an interest; or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property
by $1,000 or more per
12
month period.
Regulation 18702.3(a) (3).
Accordingly,
if decisions regarding the pending litigation
will result in changes in water allocation which will affect
Mr.
Parker’s interests in real property in the amounts stated
above,
his disqualification will be required.
Conversely,
if the “public generally” exception discussed
below is applicable to your facts,
Mr.
Parker may participate in
decisions regarding the litigation which will have an effect on
water rates.
2.
Business Interests
Mr.
Parker’s business,
in which presumably he has an
investment of $1,000 or more, will be affected indirectly by the
pending decisions.
When a relatively small business such as Mr.
Parker’s will be affected indirectly by governmental decisions,
the appropriate standard for determining materiality is that of
Regulation 18702.2(g)
which states that the effect of
a decision
is material if the decision will affect the public official’s
interests as follows:


Our File No.
1-92-449
Page
9
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for
a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more;
or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for
a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more;
or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
It does not appear from the facts you have provided that
Mr.
Parker’s business will be affected in the amounts stated above
as
a result of the pending decisions.
However,
the Commission
does not act as
a finder of fact when it issues advice.
(In re
Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
A.ccordingly,
this determination
must be made by Mr.
Parker.
Even if his business will not be affected materially,
Mr.
Parker’s disqualification is required if any decision regarding
the lawsuit will result in his personal expenses,
income,
assets
(other than interests in real property),
or liabilities increasing
or decreasing by at least
$250.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (4).)
3.
Source of
Income
Mr.
Parker’s client is also an economic interest.
This
source of
income will
be affected indirectly
by the pending
decisions.
When an individual
6
who is
a source of
$250 or more to
a public official in the preceding twelve months will
be
indirectly affected by
a governmental decision,
the appropriate
standard for assessing materiality is that of Regulation 18702.6.
This regulation states that the effect of
a decision is material
if any of the following applies:
-
(a)
The decision will affect the individual’s
income,
investments,
or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real
property)
by $1,000 or more;
or
(b)
The decision will affect the individual’s
real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under Section
18702.3 or
Section 18702.4.
For your convenience,
we are enclosing copies of Regulations
6
Please note that if Mr.
Parker’s client is
a business entity,
the standards of Regulation 18702.2 discussed above would apply.


Our File No.
1—92-449
Page
10
18702.3
and 18702.4.
If it is determined that Mr.
Parker’s source
of income will be affected materially
by the pending decisions,
Mr.
Parker’s disqualification will be required.
4.
Litigation
MWA decisions regarding the litigation will have a direct
effect on Mr.
Parker.
This is so because,
for purposes of
Regulation 18702.1(b) (2),
Mr.
Parker is
a named party in the
litigation and will
be the subject of the proceedings.
Accordingly,
if decisions regarding the litigation will result in
his personal expenses,
income,
assets
(other than real property),
or liabilities increasing or decreasing
by at least $250,
Mr.
Parker’s disqualification will
be required.
(Regulation
18702.1(a) (4);
Newman Advice Letter,
No.
A-91-515.)
Board Member Russell
1.
Water Companies
Mr. Russell is
a member of the board of directors of two
nonprofit water companies.
As stated at footnote
4,
for purposes
of Section 87103(d),
these positions are not disqualifying
Jnancial interests because these agencies are not business
entities operated for profit.
(Section 82005.)
However,
if
Mr.
Russell receives
income of
$250 or more
in
a twelve-month
period from these companies,
the companies are potentially
disqualifying economic interests.
Your facts seem to indicate that one or both of these water
companies are
named parties in the litigation.
If this is so,
the
companies would be directly involved in the pending decisions
regarding the litigation.
(Regulation 18702.1(b) (2).)
When a business entity which is
a source of
income to
a
public official is directly involved in
a governmental decision,
the effect of the decision is material
and disqualification is
required.
(Regulation
18702.1(a).)
Thus,
if Mr.
Russell receives
income from a water company which is involved in the litigation,
he must disqualify himself from participating
in decisions
regarding the litigation.
If a water company which has been a source of income to
Mr. Russell is not a
named party in the litigation,
the company
would be affected indirectly by the pending decisions.
When
a
nonprofit entity which is
a source of
income to
a public official
will be affected indirectly by
a governmental decision,
the
standards of Regulation 18701.5 apply for purposes of determining
materiality.
For your convenience we are enclosing a copy of this
regulation.
2.
Shares in Water Companies
Mr. Russell’s shares in water companies are assets.
(Einboden,
supra.)
Consequently,
Mr.
Russell must disqualify


Our File No.
1—92-449
Page
11
himself
from participating
in any of the pending decisions if the
decisions will result in an increase or decrease in the value of
these shares of at least
$250.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (4).)
3.
Real Property
Please refer to our discussion with regard to Mr.
Parker’s
real property interests for purposes of determining whether the
pending decisions will have
a material financial effect on
Mr.
Russell’s real property interests.
4.
Partnership
Mr. Russell also has an investment greater than $1,000
in a
partnership which leases land and buildings to
a shopping center.
This partnership is
a business entity operated for profit.
As
stated above,
the appropriate standards for determining
materiality when
a business entity., will
be affected indirectly by
a governmental decision are those of Regulation 18702.2.
For your
convenience we are enclosing
a copy of this regulation.
7
5.
Family Trust
Mr.
Russell has
a one—third interest in
a family trust which
owns the land where the Lucerne Valley Shopping Center is located.
You have not provided information regarding this trust.
Thus,
we
are unable to proceed with our analysis of this economic interest.
For your convenience,
enclosed please find our Barisone Advice
Letter,
No.
1-90-751,
which discusses application of the conflict-
of—interest analysis to family trusts.
Public Generally
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of
a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable
from the effect
on the public generally.
(Section 87100.)
If the decision does not affect all members of
the public in substantially the same manner, disqualification may
be required unless the effect of the decision on an official’s
economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a
significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.)
The
Please note that if Mr.
Russell has
a
10—percent interest or
greater in this partnership,
sources of income to the partnership,
such as clients of the partnership,
will be deemed sources of
income to him and are potentially disqualifying economic
interests.
(Section 82030.)


Our File No.
1-92-449
Page
12
“public” consists of all persons within the jurisdiction of the
public official.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
81.)
Therefore,
the “public,”
as applicable to the jurisdictiOn of
a member of the
board of
MWA,
are all persons within the jurisdiction of
MWA.
In l981, the Commission considered the application of the
“public generally” exception to water rate decisions with respect
to across—the—board changes in the rate schedules
(in contrast to
decisions that affect the rate structure itself)
.
The Commission
adopted the following rule
8 concerning application of the “public
generally” exception:
An investment,
interest in real property,
income or source of
income of a designated employee
shall not be
a basis for disqualification.
.
. where
such interest will foreseeably be affected only by
the decision to fix an ad valorem property tax rate
or to adopt rate schedules inwater rates or sewer
service charges,
which adjust rates
by the
same
percentage or across—the--board—factor for all
classes,
but only after the underlying rate or
charge structure has been adopted or amended.
This
provision would not apply to decisions to adopt or
adjust
a particular rate or charge structure or to
make any other change regarding water rates or
sewer service charges which does not equally affect
all classes of customers.
Thus,
where decisions concerning the litigation fall within
the narrow exception set forth above,
Mr.
Parker
may participate
in the decision.
(Peck Advice Letter,
No.
1-92-215.)
It would appear that other decisions regarding the pending
litigation will have an effect on Mr.
Parker and Mr.
Russell which
is distinguishable
from the effect on
a substantial segment of the
public.
Although changes
in water allocation will affect all
landowners in the jurisdiction,
you have not provided information
that would indicate that
a substantial number of the public holds
numerous economic interests which are substantially similar to
those of Board Members Russell
and Parker.
Accordingly,
the
“public generally” exception appears to be inapplicable to your
facts and the board members’ disqualification is required if it is
determined that the pending decisions will affect any of their
economic interests materially,
as discussed above.
8
.
.
.
,
the Memorandum to the Commission regarding the Conflict of
Interest Code for East
Bay Municipal Utility District dated
August 25,
1981,
and the Minutes for the August 31,
1981,
Commission Meeting.


Our File
No.
1-92—449
Page
13
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do
not hesitate to call
me at
(916)
322—59Ol.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH:BMB:dl
Enclosures
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lO per page.

sections.question:
Under the provisions of the Act,
may Board Members Russell
and Parker participate

Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the

Foreseeability
The effect of
a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
To be
foreseeable,
the effect of
a decision must be more than a mere
possibility;
however,
certainty is not required.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development
Comm.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989—
991; Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.App.3d 817;
In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest.
(Witt v.
Morrow,
suora at 823.)
The pending litigation will affect the water rights and the
cost of water for all property owners in the area affected by the
litigation.
Both board members
own land which will be affected by
the outcome of the pending litigation.
Mr.
Russell also has
investments in water companies which will be affected by the
outcome of the litigation.
In addition,
Mr.
Parker is
a cross—
defendant in the litigation and may have to incur costs in
defending the lawsuit.
Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable
that decisions regarding the pending litigation will have an
effect on the board members’
economic interests.
If the effect is
material, disqualification will be required unless the “public
generally” exception applies.
Materiality
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official’s economic interest in
a decision is
materially affected by a decision.
If the official’s economic
interest is directly involved in the decision,
Regulation 18702.1
provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality.
Regulation 18702.1(b)
states that a person or business entity is
directly involved in a decision before an official’s agency when
that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will
be made by filing an application,
claim,
appeal,
or similar request or;

8
(2)
Is
a
named party in,
or is the subject
of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official’s agency.
(3)
A person or business entity is the
subject of
a proceeding if
a decision involves the
issuance,
renewal,
approval, denial or revocation
of any license,
permit,
or other entitlement to,
or
contract with,
the subject person or business
entity.
Emphasis added.

on

in which

by $1,000 or more per

Regulation 18702.3(a) (3).

Mr.

Public Generally
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of

same as the effect on a
significant segment of the public.

“public” consists of all persons within the jurisdiction of the

(in contrast to

8 concerning application of the “public
generally” exception:
An investment,
interest in real property,
income or source of

shall not be
a basis for disqualification.
.
. where
such interest will foreseeably be affected only by
the decision to fix an ad valorem property tax rate

service charges,
which adjust rates
by the
same
percentage or across—the--board—factor for all
classes,
but only after the underlying rate or
charge structure has been adopted or amended.
This
provision would not apply to decisions to adopt or
adjust
a particular rate or charge structure or to
make any other change regarding water rates or

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission October 21, 1992 Amy Greyson BRUNICK, ALVAREZ & BATTERSBY 1839 Commercenter West Post Office Box 6425 San Bernardino, CA 92412 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. 1—92—449 Dear Ms. Greyson: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the responsibilities of Mojave Water Agency Board of Directors members John Russell and George Parker under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 Your request is general in nature and does not refer to a pending decision before the members of the board. Accordingly, we treat your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.2 QUESTION Under the provisions of the Act, may Board Members Russell and Parker participate in governmental decisions regarding pending litigation? CONCLUSION Under the provisions of the Act, Board Members Russell and Parker may not participate in governmental decisions regarding 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et g. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1-92-449 Page 2 pending litigatjo if the decisions will have a material financial effect on any of their economic interests, as discussed below. John Russell and George Parker are members of the Board of Directors of the Mojave Water Agency (HMWAII), a public agency. 1. Litigatj In Nay, 1990, a complaint for declaratory and Ifljunctjv relief, and a petition for writ of mandamus were filed in the San Bernardino County Superior Court entitled City of arstow and the one of 150 defendansjnis action. By their action, the plaintiffs who use water from the Nojave River System, sought to have the court determine the amounts of water from the Mojave River System that the City of Barstow and Southern California Water Company are entitled to have flowing to them through the Barstow Gauging Statjo. In June, 1991, MWA filed a cross_complaint entitled ve v. Anderson et a).., in the above action. A first amended cross_complaint was then filed in July, 1991. The named cross_defendants in the cross_complaint include Board Member Parker. The purpose of MWA’s cross_complaint is to obtain an adjudicaj 0 of water rights Within MWA and the Mojave River area of influence. This adjudjcatio will affect the rights of all persons who own Property in the area affected and who will extract water for their personal or business use. Each extractor of water will have to pay for replacement water to recharge what they produce in excess of safe yield. The water rights of a cross- defendant may be diminished to a Percentage of what he or she claims at
